MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This Question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Question paper is divided into FIVE Sections - Section A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A - Question Number 1 to 18 are Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) and Question Number 19 and 20 are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B - Question Number 21 to 25 are Very Short Answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C - Question Number 26 to 31 are Short Answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D - Question Number 32 to 35 are Long Answer (LA) type questions, carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E - Question Number 36 to 38 are case study based questions, carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section - B, 3 questions in Section - C, 2 questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{D}{/tex} and {tex}{2}{/tex} questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{E}{/tex}.

SECTION - B

In this section there are 5 very short answer type questions of {tex}{2}{/tex} marks each.
21.

The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 6 cm3/s. How fast is the surface area of cube increasing, when the length of an edge is 8 cm?

Solution:

Given, {tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=6\ {cm}^{3} / {sec}{/tex}. Since, {tex}V=x^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d V}{d t}=3 x^{2} \frac{d x}{d t} \Rightarrow 6=3 x^{2} \frac{d x}{d t} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{2}{x^{2}} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex}
Now, Surface Area {tex}={S}=6 x^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{d S}{d t}=12 x \frac{d x}{d t}=3 {~cm}^{2} / {sec}{/tex}

22.(i)

Express the simplest form: tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1-\sin x}\right),-\frac{\pi}{2}<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

Solution:

We have,
{tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1-\sin x}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)}{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}}{\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{1+\tan \frac{x}{2}}{1-\tan \frac{x}{2}}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)\right\}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex}{tex}\left[\because-\frac{\pi}{2}<x<\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{4}<\frac{x}{2}<\frac{\pi}{4}\right.{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow 0<\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}<\frac{\pi}{2} ]{/tex}

 2

OR
(ii)

Find the principal value of {tex}\tan ^{-1}(1)+\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}\tan ^{-1}(1)+\left[\pi-\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right]-\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}

23.

Show that {tex}{f}(x)=\frac{4 \sin x}{2+\cos x}-x{/tex} is an increasing function of {tex}x{/tex} in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}f(x)=\frac{4 \sin x}{2+\cos x}-x \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\cos x(4-\cos x)}{(2+\cos x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\text {when} x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right], \cos x \geq 0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\cos x(4-\cos x)}{(2+\cos x)^{2}} \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\text { Thus, } f \text { is increasing on }\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right].{/tex}

24.(i)

If {tex}y=\cos ^{3}\left(\sec ^{2} 2 t\right){/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}y=\cos ^{3}\left(\sec ^{2} 2 t\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=3 \cos ^{2}\left(\sec ^{2} 2 t\right)\left[-\sin \left(\sec ^{2} 2 t\right)\right] {/tex} {tex}\times \frac{d\left(\sec ^{2} 2 t\right)}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=-3 \cos ^{2}\left(\sec ^{2} 2 t\right) \cdot \sin \left(\sec ^{2} 2 t\right) {/tex} {tex}\times 2 \sec 2 t \cdot \sec 2 t \tan 2 t \cdot 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d t}=-12 \cos ^{2}\left(\sec ^{2} 2 t\right) \times \sin \left(\sec ^{2} 2 t\right) {/tex} {tex}\times \sec ^{2} 2 t \times \tan 2 t{/tex}

 2

OR
(ii)

If {tex}{x^y} = {e^{x - y}},{/tex} prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\log x}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

Solution:

We have {tex}{x^y} = {e^{x - y}}{/tex} 

Taking logarithm on both sides, we get

{tex}y\log x = x - y{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y\left( {1 + \log x} \right) = x{/tex}

i.e. {tex}y = \frac{x}{{1 + \log x}}{/tex}

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right).1 - x\left( {\frac{1}{x}} \right)}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{\log x}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

25.

Evaluate: {tex}\int_{\frac{-1}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}} \cos x \cdot \log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right) {d} x{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}\text { Let } f(x)=\cos x \cdot \log \left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right){/tex}
So, {tex}f(-x)=\cos (x) \cdot \log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)=-f(x){/tex} [Odd function]
Thus, {tex}I=\int_{\frac{-1}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}} \cos x \cdot \log \left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right) d x=0{/tex}

26.

Given that {tex}x^{y}+y^{x}=a^{b}{/tex}, where {tex}a{/tex} and {tex}b{/tex} are positive constants, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.

Solution:

Let {tex}{u}=x^{y} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{y}\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right), {/tex} {tex}{v}={y}^{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=y^{x}\left(\frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right){/tex}
Since, {tex}{u}+{v}=a^{b} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-\left(x^{y-1} y+y^{x} \log y\right)}{\left(x^{y} \log x+y^{x-1} x\right)}{/tex}

27.(i)

Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={y} \cot 2 x{/tex}, given that {tex}{y}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=2{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=y \cot 2 x \Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int \cot 2 x\ d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log|y|=\frac{1}{2} \log |\sin 2 x|+\log c{/tex}
{tex}y=c . \sqrt{\sin 2 x}{/tex}
{tex}\text { when } y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=2, \text { gives } c=2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y=2 \sqrt{\sin 2 x}{/tex} is the required Particular solution of given D.E.

 3

OR
(ii)

Find the particular solution of the differential equation
{tex}\left(x {e}^{\frac{{y}}{x}}+{y}\right) {d} x=x {dy} \text {, given that } {y}=1 \text { when } x=1 .{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} so, its a homogeneous differential equation
Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
Now, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=e^{v}+v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-e^{-v}=\log |x|+c \Rightarrow-e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=\log |x|+c \ldots{/tex}(1)
Now, {tex}x=1, y=1{/tex}, gives {tex}c=-e^{-1}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\log |x|+e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=e^{-1}{/tex}

28.

Find: {tex}\int \frac{2 x+3}{x^{2}(x+3)} d x{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}I=\int \frac{2 x+3}{x^{2}(x+3)} d x=\int \frac{x+3}{x^{2}(x+3)} d x+\int \frac{x}{x^{2}(x+3)} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x+\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{x+3-x}{x(x+3)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x+\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{x} d x-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{x+3} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{-1}{x}+\frac{1}{3} \log |x|-\frac{1}{3} \log |x+3|+c{/tex}

29.(i)

A card from a well shuffled deck of 52 playing cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, a card is drawn at random and is found to be a King. Find the probability of the lost card being a King.

Solution:

Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} be the event of lost card is King,
{tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} be the event of lost card not a King and
A be the event of drawing a King from remaining 51 cards.
so, {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{13}, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{12}{13}, {/tex} {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)=\frac{3}{51}, {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{51}{/tex}
Now, Required probability is {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1} \mid {A}\right){/tex},
{tex}{P}\left({E}_{1} \mid {A}\right)=\frac{{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right) \times {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)}=\frac{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{3}{51}}{\frac{1}{13} \times \frac{3}{51}+\frac{12}{13} \times \frac{4}{51}}=\frac{1}{17}{/tex}

 3

OR
(ii)

A biased die is twice as likely to show an even number as an odd number. If such a die is thrown twice, find the probability distribution of the number of sixes. Also, find the mean of the distribution.

Solution:

Let {tex}{P}(1)={P}(3)={P}(5)={p}{/tex}, so {tex}{P}(2)={P}(4)={P}(6)=2 {p}{/tex}
As, {tex}{P}(1)+{P}(2)+{P}(3)+{P}(4)+{P}(5)+{P}(6){/tex} {tex}=1 \Rightarrow 9 {p}=1 \Rightarrow {p}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
P(Getting 6{tex})=\frac{2}{9}, {P}({/tex}Not getting six{tex})=\frac{7}{9}{/tex}
Let X represents the Number of sixes
Possible values of X are 0, 1 or 2
Now, {tex}{P}({X}=0)=\frac{7}{9} \times \frac{7}{9}=\frac{49}{81},{/tex} {tex} {P}({X}=1)=2 \times \frac{7}{9} \times \frac{2}{9}=\frac{28}{81},{/tex} {tex} {P}({X}=2)=\frac{2}{9} \times \frac{2}{9}=\frac{4}{81}{/tex}
Required probability distribution of number of sixes is

{tex}X{/tex} 0 1 2
{tex}P(X){/tex} {tex}\frac{49}{81}{/tex} {tex}\frac{28}{81}{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{81}{/tex}

Mean of {tex}{X}=\sum\limits_{i=1}^{3} X_{i} P\left(X_{i}\right){/tex} {tex}=0+\frac{28}{81}+\frac{8}{81}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex}

30.

Solve the following L.P.P. graphically:
Maximise {tex}{Z}=x+3 {y}{/tex}
subject to the constraints:
{tex}x+2 y \leq 200{/tex}
{tex}x+y \leq 150{/tex}
{tex}y \leq 75{/tex}
{tex}x, y \geq 0{/tex}

Solution:

On plotting the graph of {tex}x+2 y \leq 200, x+y \leq 150, y \leq 75, \& x \geq 0, y \geq 0{/tex} we get the following graph and common shaded region is the region ABCDE.

Now, Corner points of the common shaded region are {tex}{A}(0,75), B(50,75), C(100,50), D(150,0) \& E(0,0){/tex}. Thus,

Corner points Value of {tex}Z=x+3 y{/tex}
{tex}A(0,75){/tex} 225
{tex}B(50,75){/tex} 275
{tex}C(100,50){/tex} 250
{tex}D(150,0){/tex} 150
{tex}E(0,0){/tex} 0

So, Maximum Value of Z is 275 at {tex}x=50\ \& \ y=75{/tex}.

31.(i)

Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{x\ d x}{1+\cos 2 x+\sin 2 x}{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{x}{1+\cos 2 x+\sin 2 x} d x \ldots(1){/tex}
{tex}\text { On applying } \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\text { we get } I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\frac{\pi}{4}-x}{1+\cos 2 x+\sin 2 x} d x \ldots(2){/tex}
On adding Eq. (1) and (2), we get {tex}2 I=\frac{\pi}{4} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{1+\cos 2 x+\sin 2 x} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{\pi}{16} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x+\sin x \cos x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{16} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^{2} x d x}{1+\tan x}{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{\pi}{16}(\log \|1+\tan x\|)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{\pi}{16} \log 2{/tex}

 3

OR
(ii)

Find: {tex}\int {e}^{x}\left[\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}+\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}\right] {d} x{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}I=\int e^{x}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{Let} f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}, {/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}}{1+x^{2}}=\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
On applying {tex}\int e^{x}\left(f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right) d x=e^{x} f(x)+c{/tex}
{tex}I=e^{x} \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}+c{/tex}

32.(i)

Let {tex}{A}={R}-\{5\}{/tex} and {tex}{B}={R}-\{1\}{/tex}. Consider the function {tex}{f}: {A} \rightarrow {B}{/tex}, defined by {tex}{f}(x)=\frac{x-3}{x-5}{/tex}. Show that f is one-one and onto.

Solution:

{tex}\text { Let } f\left(x_{1}\right)=f\left(x_{2}\right), \text { for some } x_{1}, x_{2} \in A{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x_{1}-3}{x_{1}-5}=\frac{x_{2}-3}{x_{2}-5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x_{1}-3\right)\left(x_{2}-5\right)=\left(x_{2}-3\right)\left(x_{1}-5\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x_{1}=x_{2},{/tex} So f is one-one Function. 
Let {tex}y=f(x)=\frac{x-3}{x-5} \Rightarrow y(x-5)=x-3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y x-5 y=x-3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{5 y-3}{y-1}{/tex}, We observe that {tex}x{/tex} is defined for all values of y except {tex}y=1{/tex},
So, Range {tex}=R-\{1\}{/tex} and Co-domain is Given {tex}R-\{1\}{/tex} [As, {tex}f: A \rightarrow B{/tex}]
Since, Range {tex}={Co}{/tex}-domain, {tex}f{/tex} is onto Function.
Thus, {tex}f{/tex} is one-one & onto function.

 5

OR
(ii)

Check whether the relation S in the set of real numbers R defined by {tex}{S}=\{({a}, {b}){/tex}: where {tex}{a}-{b}+\sqrt{2}{/tex} is an irrational number{tex}\}{/tex} is reflexive, symmetric or transitive.

Solution:

Reflexive: For {tex}a \in S{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a-a+\sqrt{2}{/tex} is irrational number
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{2}{/tex} is irrational number
{tex}\Rightarrow(a, a) \in S{/tex}
Thus, S is Reflexive Relation.
Symmetric: Let {tex}(a, b) \in S \Rightarrow a-b+\sqrt{2}{/tex} is irrational number but {tex}b-a+\sqrt{2}{/tex} may not be irrational number
For example, {tex}(\sqrt{2}, 1) \in S \Rightarrow \sqrt{2}-1+\sqrt{2}=2 \sqrt{2}-1{/tex} is irrational number}
{tex}(1, \sqrt{2}) \notin S{/tex} as {tex}1-\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2}=1{/tex} is not irrational number
{tex}\therefore(b, a) \notin S{/tex}, So S is NOT Symmetric Relation.
Transitive: Let {tex}(a, b) \in S \Rightarrow a-b+\sqrt{2}{/tex} is irrational number {tex}\&(b, c) \in S \Rightarrow b-c+\sqrt{2}{/tex} is irrational number but {tex}a-c+\sqrt{2}{/tex} may not be irrational number
For example, {tex}(1, \sqrt{3}) \in S \Rightarrow 1-\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2}{/tex} is irrational number
{tex}(\sqrt{3}, \sqrt{2}) \in S \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2}=\sqrt{3}{/tex} is irrational number
But {tex}(1, \sqrt{2}) \notin S{/tex} as {tex}1-\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2}=1{/tex} is not irrational number
{tex}\therefore(a, c) \notin S, So \ S{/tex} is NOT Transitive Relation.
Thus, S is Reflexive But Neither Symmetric nor Transitive Relation.

33.(i)

Find the distance between the line {tex}\frac{x}{2}=\frac{2 y-6}{4}=\frac{1-z}{-1}{/tex} and another line parallel to it passing through the point {tex}(4,0,-5){/tex}.

Solution:

Equation of the given line in standard form is
{tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-1}{1}{/tex}
Equation of the line parallel to {tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} & passing through ({tex}4,0,-5{/tex}) is {tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z+5}{1}{/tex}
Vector Equation of Lines are {tex}{L}_{1}: \vec{r}=(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}{L}_{2}: \vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})-(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -3 & -6 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=9 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+14 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+4+1}=3{/tex}
Thus, distance between the lines is
{tex}\text {S.D.}=\frac{\left|\left(\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{\\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{81+256+196}}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{533}}{3} \text {units}{/tex}

 5

OR
(ii)

If the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{{y}-2}{2 {k}}=\frac{{z}-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{3 {k}}=\frac{{y}-1}{1}=\frac{{z}-6}{-7}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other, find the value of k and hence write the vector equation of a line perpendicular to these two lines and passing through the point {tex}(3,-4,7){/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 k}=\frac{z-3}{2} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_1=\langle-3,2 k, 2\rangle{/tex}
{tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-1}{3 k}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-7} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_{2}=\langle 3 k, 1,-7\rangle{/tex}
Since {tex}{L}_{1} \perp {~L}_{2}{/tex},
{tex}-9 k+2 k-14=0 \Rightarrow k=-2{/tex}
Thus, d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{1}= \lt -3,-4,2 \gt {/tex}, d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{2}= \lt -6,1,-7 \gt {/tex}
Now the vector perpendicular to both {tex}{L}_{1} \& {~L}_{2}{/tex} is given by
{tex}\vec{b}=\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -3 & -4 & 2 \\ -6 & 1 & -7\end{array}\right\|=26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}{/tex}
Thus, Equation of the required line is
{tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k})+\lambda(26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}){/tex}

34.

Use the product of matrices {tex}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 2 & -2 \\ 2 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 1 & 2 \\ -7 & 7 & -7 \\ -7 & 5 & -4\end{array}\right){/tex} to solve the following system of equations:
{tex}x+2 y-3 z=6{/tex}
{tex}3 x+2 y-2 z=3{/tex}
{tex}2 x-y+z=2{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}A B=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 2 & -2 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 1 & 2 \\ -7 & 7 & -7 \\ -7 & 5 & -4 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 7 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 7 \end{array}\right)=7 I{/tex}
Thus, {tex}{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{7} B=\frac{1}{7}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 1 & 2 \\ -7 & 7 & -7 \\ -7 & 5 & -4\end{array}\right){/tex}
so, Given equation can be written into a matrix equation as

{tex} \left(\begin{array}{l}x \\ y \\ z\end{array}\right)=\frac{1}{7}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 1 & 2 \\ -7 & 7 & -7 \\ -7 & 5 & -4\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l}6 \\ 3 \\ 2\end{array}\right)=\frac{1}{7}\left(\begin{array}{c}7 \\ -35 \\ -35\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{c}1 \\ -5 \\ -5\end{array}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \therefore x=1, y=-5, z=-5{/tex}

35.(i)

Sketch the graph of {tex}{y}=x|x|{/tex} and hence find the area bounded by this curve, X-axis and the ordinates {tex}x=-2{/tex} and {tex}x=2{/tex}, using integration.

Solution:


As, {tex}y=x|x|=\left\{\begin{array}{c}-x^{2}, x \lt 0 \\ x^{2}, x \geq 0\end{array}\right.{/tex}
Area of the shaded region {tex}=\int_{-2}^{2} y d x=2 \int_{0}^{2} y d x=2 \int_{0}^{2} x^{2} d x{/tex}
{tex}=2\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)_{0}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=2\left(\frac{8}{3}\right)=\frac{16}{3}{/tex}

Using integration, find the area bounded by the ellipse {tex}9 x^{2}+25 y^{2}=225{/tex}, the lines {tex}x=-2, x=2{/tex}, and the X-axis.

Solution:


{tex}\text { As, } 9 x^{2}+25 y^{2}=225 \Rightarrow y= \pm \frac{3}{5} \sqrt{5^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex} \text { Required Area } =\int\limits_{-2}^2 \frac{3}{5} \sqrt{5^2-x^2} d x=\frac{6}{5} \int\limits_0^2 \sqrt{5^2-x^2} d x {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{6}{5}\left(\frac{x \sqrt{5^2-x^2}}{2}+\frac{25}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)\right)_0^2 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{6}{5}\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{21}}{2}+\frac{25}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\left(\frac{6 \sqrt{21}}{5}+15 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)\right) {/tex}

36.

An instructor at the astronomical centre shows three among the brightest stars in a particular constellation. Assume that the telescope is located at {tex}{O}(0,0,0){/tex} and the three stars have their locations at the points {tex}{D}, {A}{/tex} and V having position vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, 7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. How far is the star V from star A? (1)
  2. Find a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex}. (1)
    1. Find the measure of {tex}\angle {VDA}{/tex}. (2)

      OR

    2. What is the projection of vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DV}}{/tex} on vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex} ? (2)
Solution:
  1. {tex}\overrightarrow{A V}={/tex} Position Vector of V - Position Vector of A
    {tex}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}-7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
    Thus, {tex}|\overrightarrow{A V}|=\sqrt{100+4+9}=\sqrt{113}{/tex} units
  2. {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}={/tex} Position Vector of {tex}{A}\ -{/tex} Position Vector of D
    {tex}=7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
    Unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\frac{5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{3 \sqrt{5}}{/tex}
    1. {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}\angle V D A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D V}||\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11 \sqrt{2}}{90}\right){/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
      Projection of {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}{/tex} on {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\frac{11 \sqrt{5}}{15}{/tex}

37.

Rohit, Jaspreet and Alia appeared for an interview for three vacancies in the same post. The probability of Rohit's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}, Jaspreet's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and Alia's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}. The event of selection is independent of each other.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What is the probability that at least one of them is selected? (1)
  2. Find {tex}{P}({G} \mid \overline{{H}}){/tex} where G is the event of Jaspreet's selection and {tex}\overline{{H}}{/tex} denotes the event that Rohit is not selected. (1)
    1. Find the probability that exactly one of them is selected. (2)

      OR

    2. Find the probability that exactly two of them are selected. (2)
Solution:

Given {tex}{P}({/tex}Rohit{tex})=\frac{1}{5}, {P}({/tex}Jaspreet{tex})=\frac{1}{3}, {P}({/tex}Alia{tex})=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

  1. P (atleast one of them is selected) {tex}=1-{P}{/tex} (no one is selected) {tex}=1-\left(\frac{4}{5} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{P}({G} \mid \overline{{H}})=\frac{P(G \cap \bar{H})}{P(\bar{H})}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
    1. P (exactly one of them selected)
      {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex}{tex}=\frac{6+12+8}{60}=\frac{13}{30}{/tex}

      OR

    2. P (exactly two of them selected)
      {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}({R}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{3+2+4}{60}=\frac{3}{20}{/tex}

38.

A store has been selling calculators at ₹ 350 each. A market survey indicates that a reduction in price (p) of calculator increases the number of units ({tex}x{/tex}) sold. The relation between the price and quantity sold is given by the demand function {tex}{p}=450-\frac{1}{2} x{/tex}.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Determine the number of units ({tex}x{/tex}) that should be sold to maximise the revenue {tex}{R}(x)=x {p}(x){/tex}. Also, verify the result. (2)
  2. What rebate in price of calculator should the store give to maximise the revenue? (2)
Solution:
  1. Revenue by selling {tex}x{/tex} items {tex}=R(x)=x \cdot p(x)=450 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=450-x{/tex}
    For Maxima or Minima, {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=450{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d^{2} R}{d x^{2}}=-1 \lt 0{/tex}
    (Revenue is maximum when {tex}x=450{/tex} units are sold)
  2. At {tex}x=450, p=450-\frac{450}{2}=225{/tex}
    So, Rebate {tex}=350-225={/tex} Rs. 125 per calculator

Solution: Equation of the given line in standard form is {tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-1}{1}{/tex} Equation of the line parallel to {tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} & passing through ( {tex}4,0,-5{/tex} ) is {tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z+5}{1}{/tex} Vector Equation of Lines are {tex}{L}_{1}: \vec{r}=(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}{L}_{2}: \vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})-(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -3 & -6 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=9 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+14 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+4+1}=3{/tex} Thus, distance between the lines is {tex}\text {S.D.}=\frac{\left|\left(\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{\\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{81+256+196}}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{533}}{3} \text {units}{/tex} 5 OR (ii) If the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{{y}-2}{2 {k}}=\frac{{z}-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{3 {k}}=\frac{{y}-1}{1}=\frac{{z}-6}{-7}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other, find the value of k and hence write the vector equation of a line perpendicular to these two lines and passing through the point {tex}(3,-4,7){/tex} . Solution: {tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 k}=\frac{z-3}{2} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_1=\langle-3,2 k, 2\rangle{/tex} {tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-1}{3 k}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-7} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_{2}=\langle 3 k, 1,-7\rangle{/tex} Since {tex}{L}_{1} \perp {~L}_{2}{/tex} , {tex}-9 k+2 k-14=0 \Rightarrow k=-2{/tex} Thus, d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{1}= \lt -3,-4,2 \gt {/tex} , d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{2}= \lt -6,1,-7 \gt {/tex} Now the vector perpendicular to both {tex}{L}_{1} \& {~L}_{2}{/tex} is given by {tex}\vec{b}=\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -3 & -4 & 2 \\ -6 & 1 & -7\end{array}\right\|=26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, Equation of the required line is {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k})+\lambda(26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}){/tex} 5 34. Use the product of matrices {tex}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 2 & -2 \\ 2 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 1 & 2 \\ -7 & 7 & -7 \\ -7 & 5 & -4\end{array}\right){/tex} to solve the following system of equations: {tex}x+2 y-3 z=6{/tex} {tex}3 x+2 y-2 z=3{/tex} {tex}2 x-y+z=2{/tex} Solution: {tex}A B=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 2 & -2 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 1 & 2 \\ -7 & 7 & -7 \\ -7 & 5 & -4 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 7 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 7 \end{array}\right)=7 I{/tex} Thus, {tex}{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{7} B=\frac{1}{7}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 1 & 2 \\ -7 & 7 & -7 \\ -7 & 5 & -4\end{array}\right){/tex} so, Given equation can be written into a matrix equation as {tex} \left(\begin{array}{l}x \\ y \\ z\end{array}\right)=\frac{1}{7}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 1 & 2 \\ -7 & 7 & -7 \\ -7 & 5 & -4\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l}6 \\ 3 \\ 2\end{array}\right)=\frac{1}{7}\left(\begin{array}{c}7 \\ -35 \\ -35\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{c}1 \\ -5 \\ -5\end{array}\right) {/tex} {tex} \therefore x=1, y=-5, z=-5{/tex} 5 35. (i) Sketch the graph of {tex}{y}=x|x|{/tex} and hence find the area bounded by this curve, X-axis and the ordinates {tex}x=-2{/tex} and {tex}x=2{/tex} , using integration. Solution: As, {tex}y=x|x|=\left\{\begin{array}{c}-x^{2}, x \lt 0 \\ x^{2}, x \geq 0\end{array}\right.{/tex} Area of the shaded region {tex}=\int_{-2}^{2} y d x=2 \int_{0}^{2} y d x=2 \int_{0}^{2} x^{2} d x{/tex} {tex}=2\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=2\left(\frac{8}{3}\right)=\frac{16}{3}{/tex} 5 OR (ii) Using integration, find the area bounded by the ellipse {tex}9 x^{2}+25 y^{2}=225{/tex} , the lines {tex}x=-2, x=2{/tex} , and the X-axis. Solution: {tex}\text { As, } 9 x^{2}+25 y^{2}=225 \Rightarrow y= \pm \frac{3}{5} \sqrt{5^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex} \text { Required Area } =\int\limits_{-2}^2 \frac{3}{5} \sqrt{5^2-x^2} d x=\frac{6}{5} \int\limits_0^2 \sqrt{5^2-x^2} d x {/tex} {tex} =\frac{6}{5}\left(\frac{x \sqrt{5^2-x^2}}{2}+\frac{25}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)\right)_0^2 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{6}{5}\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{21}}{2}+\frac{25}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)\right) {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{6 \sqrt{21}}{5}+15 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)\right) {/tex} 5 SECTION - E In this section, there are 3 case study based questions of 4 marks each. 36. Rohit, Jaspreet and Alia appeared for an interview for three vacancies in the same post. The probability of Rohit's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} , Jaspreet's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and Alia's selection is {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} . The event of selection is independent of each other. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: What is the probability that at least one of them is selected? (1) Find {tex}{P}({G} \mid \overline{{H}}){/tex} where G is the event of Jaspreet's selection and {tex}\overline{{H}}{/tex} denotes the event that Rohit is not selected. (1) Find the probability that exactly one of them is selected. (2) OR Find the probability that exactly two of them are selected. (2) Solution: Given {tex}{P}({/tex} Rohit {tex})=\frac{1}{5}, {P}({/tex} Jaspreet {tex})=\frac{1}{3}, {P}({/tex} Alia {tex})=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} P (atleast one of them is selected) {tex}=1-{P}{/tex} (no one is selected) {tex}=1-\left(\frac{4}{5} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}\right)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}{P}({G} \mid \overline{{H}})=\frac{P(G \cap \bar{H})}{P(\bar{H})}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} P (exactly one of them selected) {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{6+12+8}{60}=\frac{13}{30}{/tex} OR P (exactly two of them selected) {tex}={P}({R}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}(\overline{{~A}})+{P}({R}) {/tex} {tex}\times {P}(\overline{{~J}}) \times {P}({~A})+{P}(\overline{{R}}) \times {P}({~J}) \times {P}({~A}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3+2+4}{60}=\frac{3}{20}{/tex} 4 38. A store has been selling calculators at ₹ 350 each. A market survey indicates that a reduction in price (p) of calculator increases the number of units ( {tex}x{/tex} ) sold. The relation between the price and quantity sold is given by the demand function {tex}{p}=450-\frac{1}{2} x{/tex} . Based on the above information, answer the following questions: Determine the number of units ( {tex}x{/tex} ) that should be sold to maximise the revenue {tex}{R}(x)=x {p}(x){/tex} . Also, verify the result. (2) What rebate in price of calculator should the store give to maximise the revenue? (2) Solution: Revenue by selling {tex}x{/tex} items {tex}=R(x)=x \cdot p(x)=450 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=450-x{/tex} For Maxima or Minima, {tex}\frac{d R}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=450{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} R}{d x^{2}}=-1 \lt 0{/tex} (Revenue is maximum when {tex}x=450{/tex} units are sold) At {tex}x=450, p=450-\frac{450}{2}=225{/tex} So, Rebate {tex}=350-225={/tex} Rs. 125 per calculator 4
